column2: A trusted application stops working while Microsoft Defender real-time protection is active. What is a safe way to test whether Defender is involved?
column4: Temporarily deactivate real-time protection, perform the controlled test, and reactivate protection immediately afterward.
column5: Disabling antivirus increases exposure. It is a short diagnostic step, not a lasting compatibility fix.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 28
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic HighYield Defender Antivirus Scenario Source::Messer-v140

column2: Microsoft Defender is active, but its threat definitions are outdated. What security action should a technician take?
column4: Check for and install the latest virus and threat protection updates.
column5: Real-time scanning and current definitions solve different parts of the problem: scanning must be active, and its known-threat information must also be kept current.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 28
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic HighYield Defender Antivirus Updates Source::Messer-v140

column2: A connection works only while Windows Firewall is disabled. After confirming the firewall is involved, what should the technician do instead of leaving it disabled?
column4: Re-enable the firewall and configure the narrow rule or exception required for the connection on the appropriate network profile.
column5: Temporary deactivation can isolate a cause. A targeted rule restores the needed traffic while retaining protection for unrelated traffic.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 28
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic HighYield Firewall NetworkProfiles Scenario Source::Messer-v140

column2: A Windows host must accept inbound traffic identified by one required TCP port. Which firewall control should be configured?
column4: Create an inbound port rule for the required TCP port.
column5: A port rule scopes the exception to the stated protocol and port. Opening more ports or disabling the firewall would expose unrelated services.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 28
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic HighYield Firewall Ports Scenario Source::Messer-v140

column2: Only one approved Windows program should receive a firewall exception. Which type of firewall rule best matches that requirement?
column4: An application rule that permits the approved program.
column5: An application rule follows the program requirement. A broad port exception could also permit unrelated software using that port.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 28
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic HighYield Firewall Applications Scenario Source::Messer-v140

column2: A user wants one Windows sign-in connected to Microsoft services with supported settings synchronized across devices. Should the user choose a local account or a Microsoft account?
column5: A local account belongs to one device. A Microsoft account can integrate cloud services and synchronize supported settings; neither choice by itself defines whether the account has administrator rights.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 28
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic Accounts MicrosoftAccount LocalAccount Source::Messer-v140

column2: An employee needs to use ordinary applications but should not make protected operating-system changes. Which Windows account type should be assigned?
column5: Routine work should use standard privilege. A specific administrative task can be elevated separately without giving every process unrestricted rights.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 28
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic HighYield Accounts StandardUser LeastPrivilege Source::Messer-v140

column2: An application installer must add a Windows service and modify protected system files. Which level of access does the installation require?
column5: Installing a service and changing protected system files are privileged tasks. The user can keep a standard session and elevate only the installer.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, pp. 28–29
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic HighYield Accounts Administrator Scenario Source::Messer-v140

column2: A public-use Windows computer needs a temporary account with very limited local access. Which built-in account type best fits?
column5: Guest access is deliberately limited. It is a poor fit for a named employee who needs persistent settings or normal organizational access.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 28
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic Accounts Guest Scenario Source::Messer-v140

column2: On a current Windows system, should the legacy Power Users group be treated as a reliable privilege level between standard user and administrator?
column4: No. Its former elevated permissions were removed, so it is effectively similar to a standard user on current Windows versions.
column5: The group name is a historical trap. Do not select it when a task actually requires administrative elevation.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 28
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic Accounts PowerUsers Legacy Source::Messer-v140

column2: A user wants a short sign-in code associated with the local Windows device instead of typing the account password. Which log-in option fits?
column5: The device association distinguishes this PIN use from entering the account's reusable username-and-password credentials.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 28
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic Authentication PIN Passwords Source::Messer-v140

column2: A Windows sign-in must verify an inherent physical characteristic rather than something the user types. Which two biometric sign-in options listed in Objective 2.2 meet that requirement?
column5: Both are biometric options. A PIN is something the user knows, even when it is used through Windows Hello.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 28
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic Authentication Biometrics WindowsHello Source::Messer-v140

column2: A domain user should authenticate once and then reach permitted network resources without entering credentials separately for each resource. Which log-in capability provides this experience?
column5: SSO reduces repeated authentication prompts. It does not grant access beyond the permissions assigned to the user's identity.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 28
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic HighYield Authentication SSO Domain Source::Messer-v140

column2: An organization wants Windows users to sign in with facial recognition, a security key, or another Windows Hello method instead of a password. What authentication approach is it configuring?
column5: Passwordless describes the sign-in path, not unrestricted access. Windows still applies identity and authorization policy, and another factor or recovery method may exist.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 28
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic HighYield Authentication Passwordless WindowsHello Source::Messer-v140

column2: Which Windows permission layer applies to a file both when it is opened locally and when it is reached through a network share?
column5: Share permissions apply only across the network. A network request can be limited by both share and NTFS permissions, while local access is not governed by share permissions.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 29
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic HighYield Permissions NTFS Shares Source::Messer-v140

column2: A user has NTFS permission to read a folder locally but is denied by the folder's share permissions. What access will the user have through the network share?
column4: No access; the most restrictive applicable permission wins, and deny takes priority over allow.
column5: Remote access evaluates both permission layers. A permissive NTFS entry cannot override a restrictive share result.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 29
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic HighYield Permissions NTFS Shares Scenario Source::Messer-v140

column2: An administrator wants a parent folder's NTFS permission settings to propagate to its child folders and files. Which permission behavior should be used?
column5: Inherited entries come from the parent. An explicit permission set directly on a child object takes precedence over an inherited entry.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 29
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic HighYield Permissions Inheritance NTFS Source::Messer-v140

column2: A technician is signed in to Windows and needs one management application to edit protected system files. How should the application be launched?
column4: Use Run as administrator to launch that application with elevated rights.
column5: Membership in Administrators does not make every process elevated by default. Elevating the required program preserves standard privilege for routine work.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 29
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic HighYield Elevation RunAsAdministrator LeastPrivilege Source::Messer-v140

column2: Which Windows security feature presents a consent or credential boundary before software receives administrative access?
column5: UAC limits silent elevation. Its secure desktop also makes it harder for another process to automate interaction with the elevation prompt.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 29
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic HighYield UAC Elevation WindowsSecurity Source::Messer-v140

column2: Company laptops must keep the entire internal volume, including the operating system and user data, unreadable if a drive is removed. Which Windows feature should be configured?
column5: BitLocker protects a whole volume. A sign-in control alone would not protect the data after the physical drive is attached to another computer.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 29
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic HighYield Encryption BitLocker Scenario Source::Messer-v140

column2: An organization must encrypt an entire removable USB flash drive using a Windows volume-encryption feature. Which feature should it use?
column5: BitLocker To Go is the removable-volume form of BitLocker. EFS instead protects selected files or folders on NTFS.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 29
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic HighYield Encryption BitLockerToGo RemovableMedia Source::Messer-v140

column2: A user needs to encrypt only selected files and folders on an NTFS volume, without encrypting the whole volume. Which Windows feature fits?
column5: EFS works at the file-system object level. BitLocker is the choice when the entire volume must be encrypted.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 29
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic HighYield Encryption EFS NTFS Source::Messer-v140

column2: A new Windows computer must use centrally managed domain authentication and receive domain management. What Active Directory action is required?
column4: Join the computer to the Active Directory domain.
column5: Joining establishes the computer as a domain participant. A workgroup device remains independently managed even if it can reach resources on the network.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 29
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic HighYield ActiveDirectory Domain CentralizedManagement Source::Messer-v140

column2: An administrator wants a defined sequence of tasks to run automatically when members of a department sign in. Which Active Directory setting should be assigned?
column4: A log-in script assigned through Group Policy to the appropriate users, group, or organizational unit.
column5: The script performs sign-in automation. Group Policy provides the assignment mechanism and scope.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 30
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic ActiveDirectory LoginScripts Automation Source::Messer-v140

column2: An administrator wants to organize Active Directory users and computers by department so policy can be applied to that hierarchy. What should be created?
column5: OUs provide an administrative hierarchy and policy scope. Security groups are better suited to assigning permissions based on membership.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 30
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic ActiveDirectory OrganizationalUnits GroupPolicy Source::Messer-v140

column2: A computer object belongs under a different departmental OU. In Active Directory Users and Computers, what should the administrator do?
column4: Move the computer object to the target organizational unit.
column5: Moving the object changes its place in the administrative hierarchy and can change which OU-scoped policies apply. It does not copy or recreate the computer account.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 30
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic ActiveDirectory OrganizationalUnits ADUC Source::Messer-v140

column2: Each domain user needs an assigned personal network location that administrators can manage and back up centrally. What should be assigned to the user profile?
column5: A home folder is the user's assigned network storage location. Folder redirection instead changes the storage location of known Windows folders such as Documents or Desktop.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 30
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic ActiveDirectory HomeFolders Storage Source::Messer-v140

column2: An administrator must enforce a password security requirement for domain users from a central policy. Which Active Directory feature should be applied?
column5: Group Policy centrally configures user and computer settings. A log-in script runs after sign-in and is not the control that enforces an authentication rule.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 30
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic HighYield ActiveDirectory GroupPolicy PasswordPolicy Source::Messer-v140

column2: Many users need the same access to a network resource, and membership will change over time. What is the efficient Active Directory permission approach?
column4: Assign the resource permissions to a security group, then add or remove users from that group.
column5: Group-based assignment avoids repeatedly editing the resource permissions for individual users. OU placement organizes and scopes policy; it is not a substitute for the security group permission assignment.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 30
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic HighYield ActiveDirectory SecurityGroups Permissions Source::Messer-v140

column2: An organization wants each user's Documents folder to store its contents on a network share while keeping the familiar Documents path. What should be configured?
column5: Folder redirection changes the backing location of a known Windows folder. A home folder is a separately assigned personal network location.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.2; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 30
column10: A+::220-1202::2.2 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::WindowsOSSecuritySettings Basic HighYield ActiveDirectory FolderRedirection GroupPolicy Source::Messer-v140
